patient.instructions: You are LiXX’s caregiver speaking for a 7-year-old girl. Be cooperative but brief; the child makes mostly sounds and few words. Start by volunteering: over the last year she stopped using words, now repeats hand-wringing/rubbing, is more unsteady and trips, and has sudden crying/upset spells without clear triggers. If asked, add: breath-holding or bursty breathing when excited, broken sleep with waking/screaming, brief staring spells with no response then back to normal, sensitivity to loud noises, and less normal toy play (mouthing/dropping). Do not mention unless specifically prompted: low pain response, constipation/straining, cold hands/feet, teeth grinding, chewing trouble/coughing with thin liquids. Only if directly asked about toileting/behavior, reluctantly admit occasional stool smearing during diaper changes; act embarrassed. Express worry it’s a brain infection/encephalitis: mention a fever a few weeks ago with a day of being “out of it,” and a mosquito-heavy camping trip; say you read about Japanese encephalitis and think it could be a virus. Voice cost concerns: genetic testing/specialists feel like optional extras; assume insurance won’t cover much therapy and consider stopping if no obvious progress. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge and calm slightly, but stay minimal.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Atypical Rett syndrome
required_evidence: Neurodevelopmental disorder with developmental delay and regression (loss of acquired skills) after a period of early development
Stereotyped hand movements (e.g., hand wringing/washing) and/or loss of purposeful hand use
Communication impairment (loss of spoken language or marked expressive language limitation)
Gait abnormalities or truncal hypotonia/ataxia
Seizures and/or breathing irregularities may be present

No evidence of acute febrile encephalitis (to argue against Japanese encephalitis): absence of acute fever, meningismus, acute altered mental status with rapid progression, or focal neurologic deficits suggestive of encephalitis
Supportive testing plan: confirmatory genetic testing for MECP2 (and if negative, consider CDKL5/FOXG1 depending on phenotype)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal; may be low if comorbid iron deficiency from poor intake
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Usually normal; may be low (microcytosis) if iron deficiency present
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Often normal; may be low if iron deficiency present
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Typically normal; may be low if chronic hyperventilation episodes cause respiratory alkalosis with compensatory changes (not reliably captured on CMP alone)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Typically normal (may be low due to low muscle mass)

medications: No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy; treat symptomatically based on comorbidities (e.g., seizures, spasticity, GERD, constipation) and specialist guidance
non_pharmacological: Refer to pediatric neurology and clinical genetics for diagnostic confirmation and phenotype-specific workup
Order/arrange MECP2 genetic testing; consider CDKL5/FOXG1 testing if MECP2 negative and clinical features suggest
Seizure assessment: EEG if spells or regression suggests epilepsy; initiate antiseizure therapy only if seizures confirmed
Therapies: physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech/augmentative communication evaluation, and individualized education plan
Nutrition and swallowing evaluation (speech/swallow), growth monitoring; address feeding difficulties and aspiration risk
Screen and manage scoliosis; orthopedic referral if indicated
Assess breathing irregularities, sleep issues; consider sleep study if clinically indicated
Assess cardiac risk: obtain baseline ECG to screen for QT prolongation and arrhythmia risk common in Rett spectrum
Family support: connect to Rett resources, social work, caregiver support
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology/genetics follow-up within 2–4 weeks; earlier (same day/ED) if acute fever, rapidly worsening mental status, new focal deficits, or concern for encephalitis

must_include: Atypical Rett syndrome is a neurodevelopmental condition characterized by developmental delay and regression with stereotyped movements and communication/gait difficulties
It is usually related to a genetic change (often MECP2) and is not caused by routine infections or vaccines
Why Japanese encephalitis is less likely here: Rett is chronic/developmental, whereas encephalitis is typically acute with fever and rapid neurologic decline
There is no single curative medication; care focuses on symptom management and maximizing function

Key components: neurology/genetics evaluation, therapy services (PT/OT/speech), seizure evaluation and treatment if present, nutrition/swallow support, scoliosis screening, and ECG screening for QT issues
Clear plan for what tests/referrals will happen next and expected timelines
Discuss prognosis as variable; emphasize supportive care can improve quality of life and function
Review red flags requiring urgent evaluation (fever with altered mental status, new seizures, persistent vomiting, dehydration, breathing pauses/cyanosis)
Acknowledge caregiver burden and offer resources/support services
Home safety and mobility support; fall prevention and supervision
Nutrition strategies and constipation prevention (hydration, fiber as tolerated, feeding plan per swallow assessment)
Importance of keeping vaccinations up to date and routine preventive care

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history to support atypical Rett syndrome vs mimics: developmental trajectory/regression, hand stereotypies, loss of purposeful hand use, language/social changes, gait changes, seizures, breathing spells, sleep, feeding/swallow, constipation, scoliosis symptoms, prior acute febrile encephalopathy, vaccination/travel exposures, family history, prior genetic testing.

Order targeted evaluation/referrals typically needed in suspected atypical Rett: genetic testing (MECP2 first; consider CDKL5/FOXG1 depending phenotype), EEG if seizures or spells, ECG for QT screening, swallow evaluation/nutrition assessment, spine evaluation for scoliosis; consider basic labs only if clinically indicated (e.g., nutrition, anemia) rather than routine extensive infectious workup.
Review any available results (prior records, EEG/ECG/genetics if already done) to refine diagnosis and management plan; if results are pending, state expected timelines and interim safety guidance.

Make or communicate the working diagnosis of atypical Rett syndrome based on chronic neurodevelopmental regression pattern and characteristic features; explicitly address why acute encephalitis (e.g., Japanese encephalitis) is less likely given time course and lack of acute febrile rapid decline.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis (including genetics and vaccine/infection misconceptions), explain treatment (no cure; multidisciplinary symptom-focused plan with clear next steps/timelines), address caregiver concerns (variable prognosis, red flags, support resources), and lifestyle guidance (home safety, nutrition/constipation, vaccines/preventive care).

Only if a symptom-targeted medication is indicated (e.g., constipation regimen, seizure rescue med, reflux treatment) and after completing required safety checks (allergy, interactions, contraindications). If no medication is needed, omit prescribing.

Arrange follow-up and referrals with timelines (e.g., neurology/genetics within weeks; therapies ongoing; ECG/EEG scheduling; nutrition/swallow; orthopedics for scoliosis screening) and provide contingency plan for red flags.
Close encounter after confirming caregiver understanding, providing written plan/resources, and ensuring safety-net instructions.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 49-year-old self-employed auto body shop owner. You are vague and a bit evasive, worried about cost and missing work. Open with: “I keep getting these scary episodes where my heart races and I can’t catch my breath.” If asked what it feels like, volunteer: sudden pounding heartbeat out of nowhere, shortness of breath, chest tightness/pressure, shaky/sweaty, intense anxiety like something bad will happen. If asked for details, say episodes last 10–30 minutes, sometimes lightheaded (no full faint), tingling in hands/lips, occasional nausea, poor sleep from worry, can happen at rest, sometimes flutter/skips between episodes, headaches after bad ones.

Misconceptions to express early: you think it’s “just panic attacks,” maybe low blood sugar (sweets sometimes help), acid reflux, or an allergy.

Withhold unless directly asked: thirst/urination, unintentional weight loss, heat intolerance, loose stools, irritability.

Resist admitting unless asked specifically and reassured: daily energy/pre-workout/caffeine, occasional friend’s stimulant pill, heavier weekend alcohol; mention embarrassment about ER.

If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but stay cautious about expensive tests and referrals.

confounders: SGLT2 inhibitor use (empagliflozin) increasing risk of euglycemic or mixed ketoacidosis and masking severity of hyperglycemia
Recent reduced oral intake/dehydration (can worsen ketosis and elevate heart rate)
Intermittent NSAID use (ibuprofen) potentially affecting renal perfusion during dehydration
Mild leukocytosis and low-grade temperature that could suggest infection but may be stress response
Underlying obesity and hypertension increasing baseline dyspnea/tachycardia and complicating assessment

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute gastroenteritis with hypovolemic dehydration causing pre-renal acute kidney injury
acceptable_alternatives: Pre-renal azotemia due to volume depletion
required_evidence: Clinical history of acute vomiting and/or diarrhea with reduced oral intake
Signs of volume depletion (e.g., orthostatic symptoms, tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, decreased urine output)
Laboratory pattern consistent with dehydration/pre-renal AKI (elevated BUN with relatively preserved bicarbonate unless severe diarrhea; possible mild electrolyte abnormalities)
No evidence of primary hematologic process on CBC (no severe anemia or thrombocytopenia) and no alternative explanation for azotemia provided by available labs

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (stress leukocytosis) without marked left shift
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Normal to mildly elevated due to hemoconcentration
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often low-normal to mildly low (hypotonic fluid intake) or mildly high (free water loss); overall consistent with dehydration
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Often low with vomiting/diarrhea; may be normal early
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Often low with vomiting (hypochloremia) or normal/low with diarrhea depending on predominant losses
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low-normal to low if diarrhea predominates (non-anion gap metabolic acidosis); may be high-normal if vomiting predominates (metabolic alkalosis)
expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated (pre-renal azotemia), disproportionate to other available labs

medications: Ondansetron ODT 4 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea/vomiting
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain/fever (max 3,000 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Oral rehydration solution (ORS) in small frequent sips; advance diet as tolerated (bland foods)
If unable to tolerate PO or evidence of moderate/severe dehydration: isotonic IV fluids (0.9% normal saline) bolus 1,000 mL IV once, reassess; repeat as needed based on vitals/urine output
Avoid NSAIDs during dehydration/AKI risk; avoid alcohol and high-sugar drinks that can worsen diarrhea
Monitor urine output and symptoms; seek urgent care for persistent vomiting, inability to keep fluids down, severe weakness/syncope, blood in stool/vomit, or worsening abdominal pain
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24–48 hours (sooner same day/ED if unable to maintain hydration or if worsening); repeat basic labs including BUN/creatinine and electrolytes after rehydration if AKI suspected

must_include: Symptoms and labs suggest dehydration from acute gastroenteritis leading to reduced kidney blood flow (pre-renal azotemia)
Most cases are self-limited; main risk is dehydration and electrolyte imbalance
Red flags that would change concern (blood in stool/vomit, severe abdominal pain, persistent fever, confusion, fainting, very low urine output)
Primary treatment is rehydration (ORS; IV fluids if unable to drink or severe dehydration)
How to use ondansetron and when to stop/seek care if symptoms persist
Avoid NSAIDs during dehydration due to kidney injury risk
Review allergies (penicillin causes hives; shellfish causes GI upset) and confirm no exposure in proposed meds
Discuss when antibiotics are not indicated (no evidence provided for invasive bacterial infection) and when they might be considered if red flags develop
Shared decision-making on outpatient vs urgent evaluation based on hydration status and ability to tolerate fluids
Hydration strategy: small frequent sips, ORS preferred, avoid high-sugar beverages
Hand hygiene and food safety to reduce transmission/recurrence
Return-to-activity guidance once hydration and symptoms improve

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history to assess dehydration severity, infectious features, exposures, comorbidities, and medication risks before deciding outpatient vs urgent care.
If moderate/severe dehydration or concern for kidney injury/electrolyte imbalance, order basic labs (BMP/CMP) and urinalysis to evaluate pre-renal azotemia and electrolytes.
Review labs to support dehydration with pre-renal azotemia (e.g., elevated BUN/Cr ratio) and identify electrolyte abnormalities that would change management.
Synthesize symptoms and any labs to diagnose likely acute gastroenteritis with dehydration causing reduced renal perfusion (pre-renal azotemia) and rule out red-flag alternate diagnoses.
Critical safety step before prescribing: confirm reported allergies (penicillin hives; shellfish GI upset) and ensure proposed meds do not contain related allergens/excipients.
Non-critical safety step before prescribing: screen for interactions (e.g., ondansetron with QT-prolonging agents, serotonergic agents) and adjust plan if needed.

Provide evidence-based symptomatic treatment (e.g., ondansetron) and rehydration plan; avoid nephrotoxic meds (NSAIDs) during dehydration; consider IV fluids/urgent evaluation if unable to tolerate PO.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment, red flags, hydration strategy, infection control, and return-to-activity guidance; address antibiotic expectations and shared decision-making.
Arrange timeframe-based follow-up and clear escalation plan based on symptom trajectory, urine output, and ability to hydrate.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 62-year-old woman who is anxious but speaks clearly. Start with: “I keep getting this crampy pain under my right ribs after I eat, especially greasy food, and it’s happening more often.” Volunteer: post-meal crampy RUQ pain, nausea with attacks, and feeling more tired.

If asked, add: pain can radiate to right shoulder blade; a couple times your eyes looked a little yellow for a day; intermittent dark urine; generalized itching without rash; early fullness, lower appetite, a few unintentional pounds lost; intermittent loose stools; brief flushing episodes.

Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about stool color, night sweats, daily dull right-sided ache between attacks, new food intolerance, or easy bruising.

Resist discussing urgent diarrhea accidents and low sex drive unless asked directly and privately; then admit, embarrassed.

Express misconceptions: think it’s reflux/stomach bug/stress/pulled muscle. Emphasize cost worries and fear of missing work; you may hesitate about tests. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, share more, and ask what’s necessary and affordable.

medications: Lanreotide depot 120 mg deep SC every 4 weeks
Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3 g/day)
comorbidities: Thymic neuroendocrine tumor (history of metastatic disease on somatostatin analog therapy)
allergies: Iodinated IV contrast (prior immediate hives; requires premedication)
confounders: Proton pump inhibitor use (pantoprazole) can falsely elevate chromogranin A
Cholelithiasis can mimic biliary colic and contribute to cholestatic LFT pattern
Somatostatin analog therapy can cause gallstones and biliary symptoms
Mild cholestasis can occur from intermittent biliary obstruction, complicating attribution of jaundice to tumor
Prediabetes and overweight may contribute to nonspecific fatigue and abdominal discomfort

correct_diagnosis.primary: Gallbladder neuroendocrine tumor (NET), well-differentiated grade 1/2
acceptable_alternatives: Well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor of the gallbladder (G1/G2)
required_evidence: Histopathology confirming well-differentiated neuroendocrine neoplasm arising in gallbladder
Tumor grading by Ki-67 index and/or mitotic count consistent with grade 1 or grade 2
Immunohistochemistry supportive of NET (e.g., synaptophysin and chromogranin positivity)
Staging evaluation documenting extent of disease (localized vs regional vs metastatic) with cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI) and consideration of somatostatin receptor imaging

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be mildly low if chronic disease or occult blood loss (nonspecific)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Creatini: Typically normal (important to assess renal function prior to contrast imaging)
expected_lab_results.Liver Panel - AST: Often normal in localized disease; may be elevated with biliary obstruction or hepatic involvement
expected_lab_results.Liver Panel - ALT: Often normal in localized disease; may be elevated with biliary obstruction or hepatic involvement

medications: Octreotide LAR 20-30 mg IM every 4 weeks (if unresectable/metastatic and somatostatin receptor–positive or symptomatic functional tumor)
Lanreotide 120 mg deep SC every 4 weeks (alternative to octreotide for unresectable/metastatic disease)
non_pharmacological: Confirm pathology review with Ki-67 and margins; obtain complete staging (contrast-enhanced CT or MRI abdomen/pelvis; consider chest imaging; consider somatostatin receptor PET such as 68Ga-DOTATATE based on availability and clinical question)

Surgical management for localized disease: cholecystectomy if incidentally found and low-risk; consider extended cholecystectomy with hepatic wedge resection (segments IVb/V) and regional lymphadenectomy if tumor >1 cm, invasion beyond lamina propria/muscularis, positive margins, lymphovascular invasion, or radiographic nodal disease (final approach individualized by stage and surgical oncology)
Multidisciplinary tumor board (surgical oncology, hepatobiliary surgery, medical oncology, pathology, radiology, nuclear medicine) for staging- and grade-directed plan

If metastatic or unresectable: consider liver-directed therapy (embolization/ablation) for liver-dominant disease; consider systemic options (e.g., everolimus) and peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT) when SSTR-positive and appropriate
Surveillance after definitive resection: periodic cross-sectional imaging and clinical review; frequency individualized by stage/grade (commonly every 3-6 months initially, then spaced out)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Oncology/surgical oncology follow-up within 1-2 weeks to review final pathology and staging plan; imaging-based surveillance or treatment initiation thereafter based on stage

must_include: This is a neuroendocrine tumor arising in the gallbladder; grade 1/2 means well-differentiated and typically slower growing than high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma
Grade (Ki-67/mitotic rate) and stage (spread to nodes/liver/other sites) determine prognosis and treatment
Many gallbladder NETs are found incidentally after gallbladder surgery; symptoms may be absent or nonspecific
If localized, surgery is the main curative treatment; additional liver/lymph node surgery may be recommended depending on tumor size, depth of invasion, margins, and nodes
If disease is unresectable/metastatic, treatment aims to control growth and symptoms (somatostatin analogs; other systemic or liver-directed options; PRRT when appropriate)
Need for staging scans and pathology confirmation before finalizing the plan

Review allergy history: penicillin causes urticarial rash; iodinated IV contrast caused immediate hives—contrast imaging may require premedication or alternative imaging (MRI/non-contrast when feasible)
Discuss potential side effects of somatostatin analogs (GI upset, gallstones, glucose changes, bradycardia) if these medications are used
Clarify red flags requiring urgent care (worsening jaundice, fever/chills, severe abdominal pain, vomiting, confusion)
Encourage general liver/biliary health measures: avoid excess alcohol, maintain healthy weight, balanced diet; manage diabetes if present
Medication adherence and keeping follow-up imaging/appointments are essential for surveillance or treatment monitoring
Provide a clear plan for next steps (who will call, when results will be reviewed, and how to reach the care team)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, prior gallbladder surgery/pathology context, red flags (jaundice/fever/severe pain), comorbidities (diabetes), meds, pregnancy status if relevant, and goals/concerns.
Critical safety check before ordering: confirm penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and iodinated IV contrast immediate hives; document reaction type/timing and any prior premedication.
Order baseline labs to support staging/fitness and biliary obstruction evaluation: CBC, CMP with LFTs/bilirubin, INR; consider fasting glucose/HbA1c if diabetes risk; pregnancy test when applicable.
Review labs for cholestasis/hepatitis pattern, cytopenias, renal function (affects imaging/therapy), and coagulopathy; triage urgent abnormalities.
Re-confirm contrast allergy before ordering any contrast-enhanced imaging; ensure plan for alternative imaging (MRI) or premedication pathway if CT contrast is necessary.

If not already available, request pathology confirmation details needed for grade/stage: Ki-67 index, mitotic rate, differentiation, margins, depth of invasion, lymphovascular/perineural invasion, node status; consider chromogranin A only if clinically useful and interpreted cautiously.

Order staging imaging appropriate for well-differentiated NET: MRI liver (preferred if contrast allergy) and cross-sectional imaging; consider somatostatin receptor imaging (e.g., Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT) when available for staging/therapy selection.
Integrate pathology and imaging to determine stage (localized vs nodal vs hepatic/extrahepatic metastases) and resectability.
State working diagnosis: gallbladder neuroendocrine tumor grade 1/2; specify stage/resectability as supported by results; note that final plan depends on complete staging and pathology.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (grade vs stage), explain treatment (surgery if localized; systemic/liver-directed/PRRT if metastatic), address allergy/contrast considerations, discuss SSA side effects if relevant, provide red flags and lifestyle/surveillance plan.
Critical safety check before prescribing any medication (e.g., somatostatin analog, antiemetic, analgesic): confirm allergies and prior reactions.
Non-critical but expected before prescribing: check interactions with current meds (notably agents affecting glucose, heart rate, QT risk if antiemetics, anticoagulants).

If clinically indicated based on stage/symptoms: initiate somatostatin analog for unresectable/metastatic or symptomatic functional disease; otherwise avoid unnecessary therapy and focus on referral/surveillance planning.
Arrange multidisciplinary follow-up (surgical oncology/hepatobiliary, medical oncology/NET specialist), imaging review, and a clear timeline for results and next steps.
Close with safety-net instructions, contact pathways, and confirmation of understanding.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) due to pathogenic point mutation affecting the 15q11-q13 imprinted region (paternal expression loss)
acceptable_alternatives: Prader-Willi-like syndrome due to point mutation
required_evidence: Genetic confirmation consistent with PWS due to point mutation (loss of paternal expression in 15q11-q13 imprinted region)

correct_diagnosis.primary: PPoma (pancreatic polypeptide-secreting pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor) causing extrahepatic cholestasis from biliary obstruction
acceptable_alternatives: Pancreatic head mass causing extrahepatic biliary obstruction (suspected pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor)
Extrahepatic biliary obstruction due to pancreatic neoplasm
required_evidence: Cholestatic liver injury pattern: disproportionate ALP and GGT elevation relative to AST/ALT
Predominantly direct (conjugated) hyperbilirubinemia consistent with obstructive jaundice
Preserved or mildly reduced albumin early; PT/INR normal or mildly prolonged (vitamin K malabsorption) in obstruction
Clinical syndrome described as extrahepatic cholestasis (e.g., jaundice/dark urine/pale stools/pruritus) without primary hepatocellular features
Need for confirmatory evaluation for obstruction source (e.g., RUQ ultrasound then MRCP/CT; endoscopic evaluation/biopsy as indicated)

expected_lab_results.Direct (conjugated) bilirubin: Elevated and comprises the majority of total bilirubin (predominantly conjugated hyperbilirubinemia)
expected_lab_results.Gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT): Elevated (supports hepatobiliary source of ALP elevation)
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal to mildly elevated (less than ALP/GGT rise)
expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal to mildly elevated (less than ALP/GGT rise)
expected_lab_results.Albumin: Usually normal early; may be mildly low if prolonged illness/malnutrition
expected_lab_results.PT/INR: Normal or mildly prolonged; may correct with vitamin K if due to cholestasis-related fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption

medications: Vitamin K (phytonadione) 10 mg PO/IV once daily for 1-3 days if PT/INR prolonged or prior to invasive procedures
Cholestyramine 4 g PO once to four times daily for pruritus (separate from other oral meds by 4-6 hours)
Octreotide LAR 20-30 mg IM every 4 weeks (or octreotide 100-200 mcg SC three times daily as bridge) for suspected/confirmed pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor symptom/tumor control
non_pharmacological: Urgent evaluation and relief of biliary obstruction: RUQ ultrasound first; then MRCP or contrast CT pancreas/liver; gastroenterology for ERCP with biliary stent if obstructed and symptomatic or worsening cholestasis
Oncology/surgical consultation for staging and definitive management of PPoma/pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor (resection if localized; systemic therapy options if metastatic)
Monitor for complications of obstruction (ascending cholangitis); if fever, RUQ pain, hypotension, mental status changes: treat as emergency and expedite ERCP
Nutritional support; consider fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (A, D, E, K) if prolonged cholestasis
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 24-72 hours for imaging/obstruction workup and GI referral; reassess labs in 48-72 hours or sooner if clinical worsening; oncology/surgery follow-up within 1-2 weeks after diagnosis/staging.

must_include: Labs suggest bile flow obstruction (cholestasis) with conjugated bilirubin and high ALP/GGT

Need for imaging to locate the blockage and determine cause and stage
Priority is to relieve obstruction (often via ERCP stenting) to prevent complications and improve jaundice/pruritus
Tumor-directed management depends on staging (surgery if localized; medical therapies such as somatostatin analogs if appropriate)
Symptom control options (e.g., cholestyramine for itching) and when vitamin K is used
Red flags requiring urgent care: fever/chills, worsening abdominal pain, confusion, low blood pressure, bleeding/bruising
Discuss penicillin allergy and that it will be considered if antibiotics are ever needed (e.g., for cholangitis)
Set expectations about additional tests (imaging, possible endoscopy/biopsy) and timelines

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 56-year-old woman. Start with: “I’ve had this dry cough about 5 weeks and I just can’t catch my breath like I used to.” Volunteer: dry cough x5 weeks, getting winded on one flight of stairs, more fatigue, and low-grade fevers on/off in evenings. If asked, add: chest tight/sore with deep breaths, occasional chills and mild night sweats (not soaking), a few pounds unintentional weight loss from low appetite, no improvement after urgent care antibiotics, no blood in sputum, no wheeze you’ve noticed, no sick contacts or travel. Do not mention vaping unless directly asked about smoking/vaping/substances; then admit you vape nicotine most days and seem a bit embarrassed. Only reveal hidden details if probed: worse with fast walking/carrying groceries so you’ve been avoiding activity; cough worse with exertion/talking; once or twice home pulse ox seemed low with exertion; started after a viral-like illness 6–8 weeks ago. Express misconceptions: wonder if it’s asthma/allergies or reflux (sour taste at night). Raise cost/work concerns; ask to skip CTs and prefer “cheaper/safer” inhalers/antibiotics over prolonged steroids. If clinician is empathic, thank them and become more forthcoming.

omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
albuterol HFA 90 mcg/inhalation 2 puffs inhaled every 4-6 hours as needed
ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain
confounders: recent treatment for presumed community-acquired pneumonia with incomplete response

positive low-titer ANA raising concern for connective-tissue-disease–associated interstitial lung disease
intermittent wheeze treated as asthma/reactive airway disease, potentially masking progressive dyspnea

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (COP)
acceptable_alternatives: Organizing pneumonia (secondary) pending exclusion of triggers (drug-induced, connective tissue disease, post-infectious)
required_evidence: Subacute (weeks) cough and/or dyspnea with systemic symptoms (e.g., malaise, low-grade fever) and failure to respond to empiric antibiotics
Inflammatory markers elevated (CRP and/or ESR) with low/normal procalcitonin supporting non-bacterial inflammation
No compelling alternative explanation such as bacterial pneumonia (high procalcitonin), heart failure, pulmonary embolism, or active TB
Clinical decision to treat as COP after appropriate evaluation/exclusion of secondary causes (e.g., medication exposure, autoimmune symptoms) and/or supportive imaging/biopsy if available

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (may be normal; not typically markedly high as in bacterial pneumonia)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be mildly low if chronic inflammation
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: Low or normal (argues against typical bacterial pneumonia)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Usually normal (may be mildly low in systemic illness)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Usually normal (monitor if starting systemic corticosteroids)

medications: Prednisone 0.75 mg/kg PO daily (typically 40-60 mg daily) for 2-4 weeks, then taper gradually over 3-6 months based on clinical response
non_pharmacological: Assess severity and oxygenation; provide supplemental oxygen if hypoxemic and advise activity pacing
Evaluate for and remove potential secondary causes/triggers (recent drugs, radiation, autoimmune disease features, occupational exposures)
Vaccination review and infection risk mitigation while on steroids (influenza/COVID; consider pneumococcal per local guidance)
Consider Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis if prolonged high-dose steroids anticipated (e.g., prednisone ≥20 mg/day for ≥4 weeks) using a non-penicillin regimen if needed
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks for symptom response and steroid adverse effects; then every 4-6 weeks during taper with repeat clinical assessment and inflammatory markers (and imaging/pulmonary function if available) to guide taper and detect relapse

must_include: COP is an inflammatory lung condition (not necessarily an infection) that can mimic pneumonia
Why bacterial infection is less likely (e.g., low procalcitonin/poor antibiotic response) and need to exclude secondary causes
Expected course and possibility of relapse during taper
Steroids are first-line and typically improve symptoms within days to 1-2 weeks
Planned taper duration and importance of not stopping steroids abruptly
Key steroid side effects to monitor (hyperglycemia, mood changes, insomnia, GI upset, infection risk) and when to seek care
Discuss infection risk and red flags (worsening shortness of breath, high fever, chest pain, hemoptysis)
Shared decision-making about steroid risks/benefits and alternatives if relapse/intolerance occurs
Clarify penicillin allergy and ensure any prophylactic/antibiotic choices avoid penicillins if needed
Avoid smoking and respiratory irritants; gradual return to activity as tolerated
Hand hygiene and minimizing exposure to sick contacts while on steroids
Adherence to follow-up and lab monitoring during steroid therapy

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history to support COP vs infection and identify secondary causes (drug/toxin exposure, autoimmune symptoms), severity, and baseline risks before steroids.
Order initial evaluation to support diagnosis and establish steroid baseline/safety (CBC, CMP, glucose/A1c as appropriate, inflammatory markers; consider procalcitonin if not already available).

Review objective data to reduce likelihood of bacterial pneumonia (e.g., low procalcitonin, lack of leukocytosis), assess contraindications/risks (glucose, LFTs, renal function), and guide need for further workup.
Make/confirm diagnosis of cryptogenic organizing pneumonia after considering and excluding common secondary causes and alternative diagnoses.
Critical safety check before prescribing; clarify and document penicillin allergy details and any steroid-related allergies/intolerances.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; screen for interactions with systemic corticosteroids (e.g., strong CYP3A4 inhibitors, anticoagulants, NSAIDs) and any planned adjunct meds.
Initiate first-line therapy with systemic corticosteroids with a planned taper; consider supportive measures (e.g., GI protection if indicated) and avoid penicillins if antibiotics are needed.
Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment expectations/taper, side effects and red flags, infection precautions, lifestyle guidance, and shared decision-making.
Arrange timely reassessment to confirm response within days to 1–2 weeks, monitor labs/side effects, and plan taper adjustments; reinforce relapse risk and follow-up imaging/testing as appropriate.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

minimal_paths.description: Basic COP evaluation with confirmation, safety checks, steroid initiation with taper, education, and follow-up.

ORDER_LAB: CBC, CMP, glucose (± A1c), inflammatory markers (± procalcitonin if not already available)

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “My pee has looked kind of cola-colored and my face feels puffy in the mornings.” Early in the visit, volunteer: tea/cola-colored urine for a few days, morning eyelid/facial puffiness, mild ankle swelling by evening, and more fatigue. If asked about urinary changes, add: sometimes foamy urine, peeing less despite same fluids, worse color in the morning, normal stream. If asked about other symptoms, report: dull intermittent headache, mild nausea and low appetite, higher BP reading at a pharmacy, and a sore throat ~2 weeks ago that resolved. Do not volunteer unless directly asked: 6-lb weight gain, tighter rings/sock marks, mild shortness of breath on stairs. Withhold/resist admitting NSAID use and “water pills” from a friend unless asked specifically about meds/OTC/supplements; then admit reluctantly. medications: Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg PO twice daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Recent frequent NSAID use for migraines, which can worsen kidney function and cause hematuria/proteinuria
On combined oral contraceptive, increasing baseline thrombotic risk and complicating evaluation if nephrotic-range proteinuria develops
Mild pyuria/trace leukocyte esterase without nitrites may suggest UTI despite primary glomerular process
Low-grade fever and elevated inflammatory markers could be misattributed to infection rather than autoimmune nephritis flare

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute interstitial nephritis (drug-induced nephritis)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute kidney injury due to nephritis (unspecified)
Acute glomerulonephritis (if supportive urine findings are present)
Acute tubular injury (if nephritis features are absent)
required_evidence: Acute rise in serum creatinine with reduced eGFR compared with baseline (or presumed baseline)
Clinical context consistent with nephritis/AKI (e.g., recent medication exposure, systemic symptoms) and no clear pre-renal or obstructive explanation
Electrolyte/acid-base pattern compatible with reduced renal function (e.g., hyperkalemia and/or metabolic acidosis)

expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated; BUN:creatinine ratio variable (often not markedly high unless concurrent volume depletion)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal to elevated (risk of hyperkalemia with reduced GFR)
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Normal or mildly elevated (can accompany non-anion gap metabolic acidosis)
expected_lab_results.Calcium: Usually normal (may be low-normal in significant renal dysfunction)

medications: Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 60 mg/day) for 1–2 weeks, then taper over 4–6 weeks if drug-induced AIN is strongly suspected/confirmed and no contraindications
Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate 10 g PO three times daily for up to 48 hours, then 10 g PO daily as needed if hyperkalemia is present
Sodium bicarbonate 650 mg PO three times daily if bicarbonate is low (e.g., <20 mEq/L) and patient is not volume overloaded
non_pharmacological: Stop the suspected offending drug(s) immediately and avoid re-challenge
Assess volume status; provide isotonic IV fluids only if clinically hypovolemic; avoid fluid overload
Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, IV contrast if possible) and adjust doses of renally cleared medications
Low-potassium diet if potassium elevated; review OTC/herbal supplements
Consider urgent nephrology consultation for rapidly rising creatinine, refractory hyperkalemia/acidosis, or concern for severe nephritis
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Repeat renal function and electrolytes within 24–48 hours (sooner if potassium elevated or symptoms worsen); close outpatient follow-up within 1 week after stabilization

must_include: Kidney inflammation causing reduced filtration (nephritis/AKI) reflected by higher creatinine and lower eGFR
Potential triggers, especially recent medications, and why stopping the suspected drug matters
Warning signs requiring urgent care (decreased urine output, shortness of breath, chest pain, severe weakness, palpitations)
Primary step is removing the trigger and monitoring kidney recovery with repeat labs
If steroids are used: purpose (reduce inflammation), expected duration/taper, and key side effects (hyperglycemia, mood changes, infection risk, GI upset)
If potassium or acidosis treatment is used: goal is to protect heart/metabolism and when to recheck labs
Review allergies (penicillin, shellfish) and clarify that shellfish allergy does not automatically imply iodine/contrast allergy but contrast can still affect kidneys
Discuss medication safety in kidney disease (avoid NSAIDs; check with clinician before new meds)
Shared decision-making about hospitalization vs outpatient monitoring based on severity and lab trends
Hydration guidance tailored to volume status (avoid dehydration; avoid excess fluids if swelling/shortness of breath)
Diet guidance if kidney function reduced (limit high-potassium foods if potassium elevated; moderate salt intake)
Importance of follow-up labs and keeping a list of all medications/OTC agents

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute interstitial pneumonia (Hamman-Rich syndrome)

expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) PaO2: Markedly decreased (severe hypoxemia) on room air

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit acute respiratory history and immediately screen for red flags and alternative common causes (infection, heart failure, PE, aspiration, exposures) while assessing severity and baseline function.

Order initial diagnostic workup to exclude infection and cardiogenic pulmonary edema and to assess severity/organ function (e.g., CBC, CMP, ABG/VBG, lactate, CRP/procalcitonin, BNP/troponin as indicated, blood/sputum cultures, viral PCR).
Interpret results to support acute hypoxemic respiratory failure with diffuse alveolar/interstitial process and to rule out/identify treatable mimics.

Order imaging and targeted tests to refine differential and exclude key contraindications before orders (e.g., CXR/CT chest if available, echocardiography if concern for heart failure, D-dimer/CTPA if PE concern, autoimmune serologies if appropriate).
Confirm diffuse bilateral infiltrates/ground-glass pattern consistent with diffuse alveolar damage and absence of primary cardiogenic edema; determine need for ICU-level support.
Perform critical allergy verification before any antimicrobial or other medication prescribing; document reaction type/timing (e.g., immediate urticaria/anaphylaxis vs intolerance).
Review potential interactions for planned therapies (e.g., steroids with diabetes meds/anticoagulants; macrolides/fluoroquinolones with QT-prolonging agents).
If continuing home meds (e.g., pirfenidone) or adding ICU sedatives/vasopressors, reassess interactions given hepatic risk and polypharmacy.
Reconfirm allergy status if antibiotic selection changes (e.g., escalation/de-escalation) to avoid inadvertent beta-lactam exposure in immediate hypersensitivity.
Final pre-prescribing gate for any new medication orders during stabilization, ensuring allergy documentation is complete and consistent.
Final pre-prescribing interaction screen for the finalized regimen (antibiotics, steroids, anticoagulation, sedation/analgesia).

Initiate supportive and empiric therapy appropriate to severity while mimics are excluded (oxygen escalation, empiric antimicrobials if infection not excluded, consideration of systemic corticosteroids with monitoring; avoid relying on pirfenidone for acute failure).
Assign working diagnosis of acute interstitial pneumonia (diffuse alveolar damage) once common causes are reasonably excluded; document uncertainty and ongoing evaluation.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan, prognosis/ICU needs, safety steps (allergy documentation), and invite goals-of-care discussion.
Arrange ICU/pulmonology follow-up and post-acute planning (pulmonary rehab, repeat imaging/PFTs after recovery, vaccination counseling).
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, documentation, and patient/family understanding.

expected_lab_results.WBC: Variable; may be normal or elevated depending on infection/inflammation (cannot be predicted without symptoms)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Variable; may be normal or low (anemia) depending on bleeding/chronic disease (cannot be predicted without symptoms)

expected_lab_results.Sodium: Variable; may be low with dehydration plus free-water intake/SIADH or high with free-water loss (cannot be predicted without symptoms)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Variable; may be high with renal dysfunction/ACEi/ARB or low with GI losses/diuretics (cannot be predicted without symptoms)
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): May be low with metabolic acidosis (renal failure/diarrhea) or high with metabolic alkalosis (vomiting/diuretics); variable

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)

required_evidence: Length-dependent peripheral neuropathy pattern (distal weakness and atrophy, especially feet/legs; possible hand involvement later)

Depressed/absent ankle reflexes
Foot deformities (pes cavus, hammer toes) and/or steppage gait/foot drop
Chronic, slowly progressive course with onset often in childhood/adolescence or early adulthood

Medication safety context: known susceptibility of CMT patients to severe neurotoxicity with vincristine exposure

non_pharmacological: Refer to neurology/neuromuscular specialist for confirmation (EMG/NCS) and consideration of genetic testing with counseling

Occupational therapy for hand weakness/adaptive devices and energy conservation

Podiatry/orthopedics referral for progressive deformities; consider surgical options if severe
Screen for and address complications: falls, ankle instability, scoliosis (if young), pain, sleep issues
Avoid other neurotoxic medications when possible (e.g., certain chemotherapies/agents known to worsen neuropathy) and counsel to report new/worsening numbness or weakness promptly

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 32-year-old elementary school teacher. Be cooperative and clear. Open the visit by saying: “My pee has been kind of dark and foamy, and my ankles look puffy.” Early on, also volunteer: morning facial/eyelid swelling, more fatigue for 1–2 weeks, and a small wart-like bump near your groin that’s been there for months.

If asked about urine or swelling, add: peeing less despite same fluids, rapid weight gain, rings/shoes tighter, urine sometimes tea/cola-colored (especially mornings). If asked about pain, mention a dull low back/side ache. If asked about other symptoms, mention headaches/pressure and mild shortness of breath with stairs or when lying flat.

Only reveal if directly asked about recent infections: a sore throat 2–3 weeks ago.

Withhold unless directly asked about sexual history: new partner and inconsistent condom use.

Mention mild burning with urination and that you thought it was a bladder infection; say you’ve been taking a lot of ibuprofen for a sports injury.

Express cost/time worries: ask to minimize visits/labs and prefer “just antibiotics,” assuming newer meds are unaffordable. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and become more willing to discuss testing.

medications: Omeprazole 20 mg PO once daily
confounders: Recent sore throat 2-3 weeks prior treated incompletely (raises suspicion for post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis vs other causes)
Intermittent NSAID use for headaches (can cause kidney injury and proteinuria, complicating nephritic evaluation)
Menstrual spotting at time of urine collection (can falsely elevate hematuria on urinalysis)
Cutaneous papilloma lesions with occasional bleeding (patient concerns may distract from renal symptom reporting)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute nephritic syndrome consistent with glomerulonephritis
acceptable_alternatives: IgA nephropathy (if episodic hematuria with recent URI)
Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (crescentic GN) (if rapidly rising creatinine)
required_evidence: Urinalysis with hematuria and proteinuria
Urine microscopy showing dysmorphic RBCs and/or RBC casts
Reduced eGFR and/or elevated serum creatinine/BUN consistent with kidney injury
Urine protein/creatinine ratio demonstrating non-nephrotic or variable proteinuria supporting nephritic picture

expected_lab_results.Serum creatinine: Elevated above baseline (AKI or acute on chronic kidney dysfunction)
expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated (often proportional to creatinine; may be increased due to reduced renal clearance)
expected_lab_results.Urine microscopy - RBCs: Increased RBCs; often dysmorphic in glomerular bleeding
expected_lab_results.Urine protein/creatinine ratio: Elevated (commonly >0.3 g/g; may be higher but often <3.5 g/g in nephritic syndrome)

medications: Furosemide 20-40 mg PO/IV once then titrate to effect for volume overload/edema (if present)
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily (or other non-RAAS antihypertensive) for blood pressure control if hypertensive and AKI present
Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain/fever (max 3000 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Urgent nephrology referral for suspected glomerulonephritis; consider kidney biopsy depending on clinical course/severity
Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, iodinated contrast when possible) and review all OTC/herbal products
Salt restriction (e.g., <2 g sodium/day) and fluid restriction if volume overloaded
Monitor blood pressure and daily weights; strict input/output if inpatient

Repeat renal function and urinalysis to assess trend; evaluate for systemic features (rash, arthralgias, pulmonary symptoms) and arrange further serologic workup as indicated (e.g., complements, ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM, hepatitis/HIV) per nephrology
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck serum creatinine/BUN/eGFR and urinalysis within 48-72 hours (sooner or same-day if rapidly worsening symptoms/oliguria), and nephrology evaluation urgently (within 24-72 hours depending on severity).

must_include: Kidney inflammation likely at the level of the filters (glomeruli)
Blood and protein in urine with possible decline in kidney function supports glomerulonephritis/nephritic syndrome
This can be serious and may require urgent specialist evaluation and sometimes kidney biopsy to identify the exact cause
Initial treatment focuses on protecting kidney function: controlling blood pressure, managing fluid overload, and avoiding kidney-harming medicines
Medications may be adjusted once the cause is confirmed (e.g., immunosuppression for certain types) under nephrology guidance
Need for close lab monitoring over the next days to ensure kidney function is not rapidly worsening
Review penicillin allergy history and ensure it is documented; discuss alternatives if antibiotics become necessary
Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care: decreased urine output, shortness of breath, chest pain, severe swelling, severe headache, blood pressure very high, or rapidly worsening fatigue
Discuss that omeprazole is not a treatment for nephritis and all meds (including OTC) should be reviewed for kidney safety
Avoid NSAIDs and dehydration; maintain appropriate fluid intake as advised based on swelling and kidney function
Low-salt diet and home blood pressure monitoring
Bring a list of all medications/supplements to follow-up; avoid new supplements without checking

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key nephritic syndrome history (hematuria, edema, oliguria, HTN symptoms), timeline, recent infections, systemic symptoms, pregnancy status if relevant, and full medication/OTC/supplement use.
Critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm and document penicillin allergy details (reaction type, severity, timing) and any other allergies.

Order initial nephritis workup and severity assessment: UA with microscopy (RBC casts), urine protein quantification (UPCR/UACR), BMP/creatinine/eGFR, electrolytes (K+), CBC, albumin, lipid panel if heavy proteinuria, complement (C3/C4), ASO/anti-DNase B if post-strep suspected, ANA, anti-dsDNA, ANCA, anti-GBM, hepatitis B/C and HIV as indicated; consider pregnancy test when applicable.
Interpret results to confirm nephritic pattern and assess urgency (rising creatinine, hyperkalemia, severe proteinuria, active urinary sediment).
Make working diagnosis of glomerulonephritis/nephritic syndrome and risk stratify for urgent nephrology evaluation/possible biopsy.

Targeted follow-up questions based on results: degree of urine output, dyspnea/orthopnea, chest pain, headaches/visual changes, home BP readings, weight change, recent strep/skin infection, hemoptysis (pulmonary-renal), rash/arthralgias.
Non-critical safety check prior to prescribing; evaluate interactions with current meds (including ACEi/ARB, diuretics, anticoagulants) and avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs).
If indicated for supportive care, prescribe kidney-safe management (e.g., antihypertensive/diuretic per severity and labs) only after contraindication and allergy checks; avoid nephrotoxic agents.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment plan and monitoring, address concerns (penicillin allergy, red flags, omeprazole misconception), and lifestyle guidance (avoid NSAIDs, low salt, BP monitoring, med list).
Arrange urgent nephrology referral when indicated and short-interval follow-up for repeat labs/BP/volume status (often within 24–72 hours depending on severity).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia (lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma with IgM monoclonal gammopathy)
acceptable_alternatives: IgM monoclonal gammopathy of clinical significance (if symptomatic IgM-related disease without full WM criteria)
required_evidence: History of Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia and current therapy with ibrutinib
Evidence of cytopenia pattern consistent with marrow involvement (e.g., anemia with low hemoglobin/hematocrit)
Platelet and WBC trends to assess marrow suppression vs treatment effect
Renal function assessment (creatinine/eGFR) to evaluate end-organ impact and treatment safety

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Decreased (anemia is common in WM from marrow infiltration and/or chronic disease)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Often normocytic; may be mildly macrocytic if reticulocytosis or treatment-related effects
expected_lab_results.WBC: May be normal or mildly decreased; evaluate for neutropenia if infection risk
expected_lab_results.Platelets: May be normal or decreased (thrombocytopenia can occur with marrow involvement or therapy)
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Usually normal; may be elevated if comorbid CKD or other renal involvement
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be normal or elevated in dehydration/renal dysfunction
expected_lab_results.eGFR (CKD-EPI): Usually preserved; reduced if underlying CKD/renal impairment

medications: Ibrutinib 420 mg PO once daily (continue if already prescribed and tolerated; reassess for toxicity and response)
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain/fever (max 3000 mg/day in older adults or liver risk)
non_pharmacological: Review symptoms and screen for hyperviscosity/neuropathy/bleeding/infection; urgent referral if hyperviscosity symptoms (headache, vision changes, mucosal bleeding) occur
Vaccinations per immunocompromised guidance (inactivated influenza annually; avoid live vaccines while on BTK inhibitor; ensure pneumococcal series as indicated)
Infection prevention counseling (hand hygiene, prompt evaluation of fever, avoid sick contacts when neutropenic)
Coordinate hematology/oncology follow-up for disease monitoring and response assessment
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks for lab trend review and toxicity assessment; sooner (same day/ED) if fever, bleeding, syncope, chest pain, or neurologic/vision symptoms suggest hyperviscosity or hemorrhage

must_include: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia is a slow-growing B-cell lymphoma producing IgM
Common problems include anemia/cytopenias and IgM-related symptoms (hyperviscosity, neuropathy)
Labs being checked today help assess marrow function and kidney status
Ibrutinib is a BTK inhibitor used to control WM and improve blood counts/symptoms over time
Key adverse effects: bleeding/bruising, atrial fibrillation/palpitations, hypertension, infections, diarrhea, rash
Do not stop ibrutinib abruptly without oncology guidance; adherence matters
Bleeding risk counseling (report black stools, easy bruising, nosebleeds; discuss any planned procedures)
When to seek urgent care: fever, signs of infection, chest pain/palpitations, severe headache/vision changes, neurologic symptoms
Medication review for interactions (avoid strong CYP3A inhibitors/inducers; discuss anticoagulants/antiplatelets and NSAIDs)
Avoid grapefruit/Seville oranges (CYP3A interaction increasing ibrutinib levels)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms (anemia, hyperviscosity, neuropathy), bleeding/infection symptoms, cardiovascular history, medication list (including OTC/herbals), allergies, and upcoming procedures to guide WM assessment and ibrutinib safety.

Order baseline/monitoring labs relevant to WM and treatment safety (CBC with differential/platelets; CMP with creatinine/LFTs; consider IgM level/serum viscosity if symptomatic; hepatitis B status if planning anti-CD20 therapy; pregnancy test if applicable).
Review labs to assess marrow function (cytopenias), kidney/liver status for dosing/monitoring, and red flags requiring urgent management (e.g., severe anemia, thrombocytopenia, renal dysfunction).
State/confirm Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia and identify whether symptoms suggest hyperviscosity or other complications that would alter immediate management (e.g., need for urgent plasmapheresis).
Perform required critical safety check before prescribing; confirm and document allergies (TMP-SMX maculopapular rash; penicillin urticaria) and any prior reactions to proposed drugs.
Perform required critical interaction check before prescribing ibrutinib; review CYP3A inhibitors/inducers, anticoagulants/antiplatelets, NSAIDs, supplements, and grapefruit/Seville orange intake.

If no contraindications/interactions requiring change, prescribe ibrutinib (or defer/adjust with oncology if interaction/bleeding risk is high), with monitoring plan and clear instructions not to stop abruptly without oncology guidance.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment rationale and adverse effects, bleeding/infection/arrhythmia warning signs, interaction avoidance (CYP3A, grapefruit), lifestyle guidance, and maintaining an updated allergy list.
Arrange follow-up for response/toxicity monitoring (BP, CBC/CMP, symptom review), and provide escalation plan for urgent symptoms.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: “I keep getting these bad headaches and my eyesight feels off.” Early in the visit, volunteer: worsening headaches behind your eyes for months, more frequent; side vision seems worse (bumping into things/not noticing people); nausea at worst headaches. If asked about vision, add occasional double vision (worse when tired) and light sensitivity. If asked about fatigue/weight/bowels/periods/breasts/sex, share: tired despite sleep, constipation, mild central weight gain, irregular periods 6–9 months, a couple episodes of milky nipple discharge (not pregnant), lower libido. Do NOT volunteer unless specifically asked: excessive thirst/urination (incl. nocturia), shaky/sweaty weakness when skipping meals, dry skin/cold intolerance, lightheaded on standing, reduced smell. Resist mentioning painful sex/vaginal dryness unless asked gently; then admit embarrassment and worry about hormones. Express misconceptions: “Probably migraines from stress/screens,” “Maybe I just need new glasses,” “Maybe early menopause,” “I drink lots of water so that’s why I pee.” If clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and become more willing to disclose. Raise cost/job concerns and fear of being pushed into surgery; ask about monitoring options.

medications: Metformin ER 1000 mg twice daily
Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly
Omega-3 ethyl esters 2 g twice daily
Lisinopril 10 mg daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg daily
confounders: Combined oral contraceptive use (can raise prolactin and increases hypertriglyceridemia risk; may confound amenorrhea evaluation)
Chronic stress/poor sleep from headaches (can affect cortisol axis testing)
Severe insulin resistance from familial partial lipodystrophy (overlaps with pituitary-related metabolic symptoms and complicates interpretation of weight/body composition changes)
Hyponatremia consistent with secondary adrenal insufficiency or SIADH (may mimic nonspecific symptoms such as fatigue, nausea, and headache)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pituitary region epidermoid or dermoid cyst (sellar/suprasellar congenital inclusion cyst)
required_evidence: Clinical picture consistent with sellar/suprasellar mass effect (e.g., headache and/or visual symptoms) and/or pituitary dysfunction symptoms
Pituitary hormone evaluation showing possible hypopituitarism (central hypothyroidism and/or secondary adrenal insufficiency) and/or hyperprolactinemia from stalk effect
No biochemical evidence supporting primary thyroid disease (low/normal TSH with low free T4 suggests central cause)
If polyuria/polydipsia is present: labs consistent with central diabetes insipidus (hypernatremia or high-normal sodium with high serum osmolality and inappropriately dilute urine)

expected_lab_results.Sodium: Normal to high; may be elevated if central diabetes insipidus is present
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: Normal to elevated; elevated if diabetes insipidus is present
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality: Inappropriately low/dilute relative to serum osmolality if diabetes insipidus is present
expected_lab_results.Morning cortisol (8 AM): Low if secondary adrenal insufficiency due to hypopituitarism; otherwise may be normal
expected_lab_results.ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal if secondary adrenal insufficiency is present
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal if central hypothyroidism is present
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Low if central hypothyroidism is present
expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Mild to moderately elevated from stalk effect (typically not in the very high range seen with macroprolactinoma)

medications: Hydrocortisone 15 mg PO each morning and 5 mg PO each afternoon (if secondary adrenal insufficiency suspected/confirmed)
Levothyroxine 1.6 mcg/kg/day PO daily (start only after glucocorticoid replacement if central hypothyroidism present)
Desmopressin 0.05 mg PO once to twice daily (titrate) (if central diabetes insipidus present with hypernatremia/high serum osmolality and dilute urine)
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to endocrinology for comprehensive pituitary evaluation and dynamic testing as needed (e.g., ACTH stimulation if indicated)
Neurosurgery referral for evaluation of symptomatic sellar/suprasellar cyst; consider surgical resection/decompression if mass effect (e.g., visual compromise) or progressive endocrine deficits
Ophthalmology/visual field assessment if any visual symptoms or concern for optic chiasm compression
Patient education on adrenal insufficiency sick-day rules; provide emergency steroid plan/medical alert identification if on glucocorticoids
If diabetes insipidus: counsel on free water access, monitoring thirst/urine output, and avoiding overcorrection hyponatremia with desmopressin
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Endocrinology follow-up within 1–2 weeks (sooner if abnormal cortisol/sodium or symptomatic); repeat labs (sodium/osmolality and pituitary hormones) within 3–7 days after starting or adjusting desmopressin or hormone replacement

must_include: These are typically benign congenital cysts in the pituitary region that can cause symptoms by compressing nearby structures
Possible effects include headaches/vision changes and changes in pituitary hormones (thyroid, cortisol, water balance, prolactin)
Diagnosis usually relies on pituitary labs and dedicated MRI of the pituitary/sellar region
Treat urgent hormone deficiencies first—especially cortisol deficiency—because it can be life-threatening
If thyroid hormone is low due to pituitary dysfunction, replace thyroid hormone only after starting steroids when adrenal insufficiency is possible
If water balance is affected (diabetes insipidus), desmopressin may be used with careful sodium monitoring
Definitive management may require neurosurgical evaluation for decompression/resection if symptomatic or progressive
Review red flags requiring urgent care: severe headache, worsening vision, confusion, severe vomiting, fainting, or signs of adrenal crisis
Discuss medication side effects and monitoring: hydrocortisone (infection risk, glucose), levothyroxine (palpitations if overdosed), desmopressin (hyponatremia/water intoxication)
Confirm and document penicillin allergy (hives/urticaria) and clarify reaction history
If on hydrocortisone: sick-day dosing, do not stop abruptly, and carry emergency information
If on desmopressin: drink to thirst, avoid excessive free water intake, and seek care for headache/nausea/confusion (possible hyponatremia)
Importance of follow-up labs and specialist visits to prevent complications from hormone imbalance

Review labs to determine presence of adrenal insufficiency, central hypothyroidism, diabetes insipidus, and other pituitary deficits requiring urgent treatment and to support diagnosis.

Synthesize symptoms + labs + prior/ordered imaging context to identify pituitary region dermoid/epidermoid cyst as likely etiology and document differential/need for dedicated sellar MRI and neuro-ophthalmic evaluation.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment priorities (cortisol first), address concerns/red flags, and provide lifestyle/monitoring education.
Critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm and document penicillin allergy details (hives/urticaria, timing, severity) and update allergy list.

Non-critical check before prescribing; review current meds for interactions (e.g., levothyroxine absorption interactions; desmopressin with hyponatremia-promoting drugs; steroid effects on glucose/warfarin).

Initiate indicated hormone replacement with correct sequencing and monitoring (treat suspected/confirmed adrenal insufficiency before thyroid hormone; consider desmopressin if DI with sodium monitoring).
Arrange urgent referrals and follow-up: endocrinology, neurosurgery for symptomatic/progressive lesions, neuro-ophthalmology/visual fields; plan repeat labs/sodium checks and MRI follow-up.
Close encounter after ensuring safety netting, clear plan, and follow-up instructions.

minimal_paths.description: Core safe pathway: identify pituitary cyst implications, evaluate pituitary axes, treat adrenal insufficiency first, then address thyroid/DI, and refer for definitive management.

steps: ASK: symptoms, red flags, meds, allergies, pregnancy status
ORDER_LAB: AM cortisol/ACTH, free T4/TSH, prolactin, CMP with sodium, serum/urine osmolality if polyuria; consider IGF-1 and gonadotropins
GET_RESULTS: identify adrenal insufficiency/central hypothyroidism/DI pattern
DIAGNOSE: pituitary region dermoid/epidermoid cyst with compression-related symptoms; note need for dedicated sellar MRI and visual field testing
EDUCATE: explain diagnosis and treatment priorities (cortisol first; thyroid after steroids; DI management), red flags, and monitoring
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm penicillin hives history and document
CHECK_INTERACTION: review current meds for interactions affecting levothyroxine/desmopressin/steroids

PRESCRIBE: start stress-dose or physiologic hydrocortisone if adrenal insufficiency suspected/confirmed; start levothyroxine only after steroids if central hypothyroidism; consider desmopressin if DI with sodium monitoring plan
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: urgent endocrinology, neurosurgery, neuro-ophthalmology; plan repeat sodium and hormone labs; MRI follow-up
END: safety net and return precautions

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 29-year-old male forklift operator with high-school education. Speak vaguely and a bit evasively; give short answers unless asked directly. Start by saying you keep tripping and your ankles feel weak, like your feet don’t lift right. If the clinician asks about feet shape/injuries, volunteer that your arches have gotten higher, shoes wear unevenly, you’ve had several ankle sprains from rolling, your lower legs look skinnier, and your dad had “bad feet” and wore braces.

Only if asked, mention nighttime tingling/numb toes, trouble running/stairs, heel-walking difficulty and foot slapping, hand cramps/clumsiness with buttons/jars, reduced pain/heat sensation in feet, worse balance in the dark/uneven ground, and cold feet.

Do not volunteer burns/calluses, sharp burning pains, fear of falling, or using handrails unless specifically asked about injuries, skin, pain, falls, or safety. Resist discussing public falls, avoiding social plans, and sex issues unless asked gently.

Express misconceptions: maybe a pinched back nerve, diabetes, statin side effect, or poor circulation. Emphasize worry about job documentation/costs; doubt genetics/neurology/orthoses. If met with empathy, soften slightly but stay worried about work and money.

Electromyography (EMG).value: Chronic denervation with reinnervation in distal lower-extremity muscles; no acute fibrillation potentials

Naproxen 250 mg PO twice daily as needed for pain (take with food)

comorbidities: Chronic neuropathic pain related to Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease
Recurrent ankle sprains due to distal weakness and proprioceptive loss
confounders: Borderline low-normal vitamin B12 level (can mimic peripheral neuropathy symptoms)
Regular alcohol intake (8-10 drinks/week) increasing risk of peripheral neuropathy and gait instability
History of multiple ankle sprains with mechanical instability contributing to gait abnormalities
Use of gabapentin causing mild daytime sedation and dizziness, complicating assessment of balance

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
required_evidence: Chronic, slowly progressive distal symmetric weakness and atrophy (especially feet/lower legs) with distal sensory loss
Foot deformities such as pes cavus and/or hammertoes; frequent ankle sprains/foot drop
Reduced/absent deep tendon reflexes (often Achilles) in a length-dependent pattern
Family history consistent with inherited neuropathy (autosomal dominant often, but may be recessive or X-linked)
Exclusion of common acquired causes of peripheral neuropathy using available labs (diabetes, B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, inflammatory myopathy/systemic inflammation)
Plan for confirmatory testing (not in provided labs): nerve conduction studies/EMG and genetic testing

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Within reference range (no evidence of diabetes as neuropathy cause)
expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal or mildly elevated; not in the myositis range

medications: Acetaminophen 500 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain with food (avoid if CKD, PUD, anticoagulation; max 1200 mg/day OTC equivalent)
non_pharmacological: Refer to neurology for confirmatory evaluation (NCS/EMG) and genetic counseling/testing; discuss implications for family members
Physical therapy for strengthening, stretching, balance training, gait training, and fall-prevention program
Occupational therapy for hand weakness/adaptive devices and energy conservation strategies
Ankle-foot orthosis (AFO) for foot drop; consider custom orthotics for pes cavus; supportive footwear
Podiatry/orthopedics referral for progressive deformity, recurrent ulcers, or refractory pain; consider surgical options if severe

Neuropathic pain assessment; if clinically significant neuropathic pain is present, consider guideline-based agents (e.g., gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine) tailored to comorbidities and interactions (none provided)
Avoid/limit neurotoxic medications when possible (e.g., vincristine, cisplatin, paclitaxel, amiodarone, nitrofurantoin, metronidazole, linezolid, high-dose pyridoxine); medication reconciliation
Regular skin/foot checks, especially with sensory loss; education on blister prevention and prompt care for wounds
Assistive devices as needed (cane/walker) and home safety evaluation to reduce falls
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 4-8 weeks; PT/OT initiation within 1-2 weeks; reassess symptoms, falls, pain control, and functional status in 6-12 weeks

must_include: CMT is an inherited peripheral nerve disorder causing slowly progressive weakness and sensory loss, usually starting in the feet/legs
Typical course is gradual progression; severity varies widely even within families
Explain rationale for diagnosis and why labs are used to rule out acquired neuropathy; confirmatory testing with EMG/NCS and genetics
Discuss inheritance patterns and offer genetic counseling; implications for family planning and relatives
No curative therapy; management focuses on function, safety, symptom control, and preventing complications
PT/OT and orthotics/AFOs can improve gait, reduce falls, and support daily activities
Pain management approach (start with safer options; consider neuropathic pain agents if needed) and set expectations
When to consider orthopedic/podiatry interventions
Elicit patient goals (mobility, work, sports) and concerns about progression and independence
Review red flags requiring urgent evaluation (rapid progression, new bowel/bladder dysfunction, asymmetric severe weakness, systemic symptoms, new severe back pain)
Discuss safety concerns: fall risk, foot wounds/ulcers, and driving/workplace accommodations if relevant
Daily foot/skin inspection and protective footwear; avoid walking barefoot if sensory loss
Home fall-prevention strategies (lighting, remove loose rugs, grab bars) and balance exercises as advised by PT
Avoid known neurotoxic medications when possible and inform all clinicians about CMT
Allergy reminders: penicillin causes urticarial rash; latex causes contact dermatitis—ensure non-latex equipment and document allergies

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms and timeline (slowly progressive distal weakness, foot drop, sensory loss), functional impact, pain, falls, and red flags suggesting acquired/urgent etiologies.

Clarify family history of neuropathy/foot deformities, age of onset, prior evaluations, and review medications/toxin exposures and comorbidities (e.g., diabetes, alcohol, thyroid disease) to support inherited vs acquired neuropathy.
Document allergies early (especially penicillin urticarial rash and latex contact dermatitis) to ensure safe ordering and later prescribing; use non-latex equipment.
Order screening labs to rule out common acquired neuropathy causes (e.g., A1c/glucose, B12, TSH, CMP, CBC, SPEP/IFE as appropriate) before anchoring on inherited neuropathy.
Review lab results to support exclusion of treatable acquired neuropathy and justify proceeding to confirmatory testing.
Order confirmatory testing pathway: EMG/NCS to characterize demyelinating vs axonal neuropathy and guide targeted genetic testing; consider genetic test order/referral depending on setting.
Make/communicate diagnosis of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease when clinical pattern plus exclusion of acquired causes and supportive EMG/NCS (and/or genetics) align.
Explain diagnosis, course, rationale for labs and confirmatory tests, inheritance patterns, genetic counseling, and family implications.
Explain treatment: no cure; PT/OT, orthotics/AFOs, fall prevention, pain management approach, and when to consider orthopedic/podiatry interventions.
Address patient goals/concerns; review red flags requiring urgent evaluation; discuss safety (falls, foot wounds/ulcers, driving/work accommodations).
Lifestyle education: daily foot/skin checks, protective footwear, home safety, avoid neurotoxic meds when possible, and reinforce/document penicillin and latex allergies and non-latex equipment use.
If prescribing neuropathic pain agents or other meds, check for clinically relevant interactions (non-critical but expected if meds are started).
Before any prescribing, re-check allergies to prevent exposure (critical safety step).

If symptomatic treatment is needed (e.g., neuropathic pain), prescribe using a stepwise approach with safer options first and clear expectations; avoid contraindicated/neurotoxic agents when possible.
Arrange follow-up to review EMG/NCS/genetic results, monitor function/falls/pain, ensure PT/OT/orthotics referrals are progressing, and revisit family counseling.
Close the encounter after safety netting and confirming understanding.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Open with: “I keep tripping and my ankles feel weak, especially when I walk for a while.” Also volunteer early that your feet have become more high-arched over the years and shoes fit poorly, and you’ve always been clumsy but it’s slowly worsening.

If asked about specifics, describe toe-catching/foot not lifting fully, frequent ankle sprains, nighttime tingling/numb spots, thinning lower legs, and hand fatigue with buttons/jars. If asked about family history, say one parent and an aunt had similar foot/walking problems.

Do not volunteer at first that you sometimes don’t feel cuts or hot bath water on your feet, that you avoid long walks/stairs due to fear of falling, or that balance is worse in the dark/uneven ground—share only if asked directly.

Be reluctant/embarrassed about public falls and downplay job impact unless the clinician is supportive. Mention misconceptions: you wondered about a pinched back nerve, shoes, or being out of shape. acceptable_variants.description: Performs drug interaction check earlier (immediately after medication history) and completes allergy/contraindication checks immediately before prescribing.
